Original PDF page 18

I

legendary
CHARACTERS
Original PDF page 19

Original PDF page 20

B

Legends Incarnate

eyond 20th level, a character has grown into
something very rarely seen. Their deeds carry
weight across worlds, shaping the future as
planar rulers do. The rivals they combat are
pillars of the cosmos, beings that have
endured for millennia and have entire
civilizations centered around their figures.
Legendary Characters are the apex of what an
adventurer can become, to the point where their fellow
mortals don’t see them as such anymore. The difference
between a 25th-level sorcerer and a dragon, in the eyes
of a commoner, is merely a matter of aesthetics. They
are class-defining figures, heroes of such achievement
that ambitious practitioners of their discipline might
have the stated goal of becoming like them.

Whether you are a player or a DM, this section will
help you understand, in very practical terms, the role of
players in a Legendary Campaign. Legendary
Characters are built directly on the foundation of 5e and
5.5e, extending the progression of the classes into the
Legendary Levels. If you are jumping into a Legendary
campaign without much experience at high levels, take
some time getting to know what the D&D classes are
capable of at this tier of play before the first session.

Getting Started

A Legendary Campaign begins at 20th level, either by
transitioning an existing party into the first Legendary
Tier or by building characters from scratch. Both paths
are valid–it’s up to the DM to determine which suits the
campaign best.

If you are transitioning from a regular campaign, your
characters arrive at this tier carrying everything they
have built–their history, their relationships, their hardwon abilities. That continuity is key. A Legendary
Character’s story does not begin at 20th level; it
culminates there, and the sessions that follow are about
what comes next for someone who has already changed
the world a few times.

If you are building from scratch, work closely with
your DM. You will perhaps want to establish your
character’s grand tale–the sum of their deeds,
relationships, and reputation before play begins. At this
tier, a character’s legend is part of their mechanical and
narrative identity, informing how the world responds to
them and what stakes are appropriate for their story.
Famous paladins at the head of grand orders,
archmages renowned for their arcane discoveries,
retired swordmasters sought after by every aspiring
student of the blade; what makes your character
Legendary?

Original PDF page 21

Building a Legendary Character

To begin a Legendary Campaign, you must have a 20thlevel character. Depending on the scope of your
campaign, you might want to start at a certain legendary
level. The following section will help guide you through
the process for each Legendary Tier.

When creating Legendary Characters, it is
recommended to meet up with your group and work
through the complexities of both high-level D&D and
the new Legendary Levels together. Always best to have
your fellow players and DM to help!

20th Level

Before delving into the first Legendary Tier of play, you
must have a 20th-level character ready. If you already
have one from a previous/ongoing campaign, you’re all
set! Check in with your DM to determine any additional
features—such as magic items and special abilities—and
to establish your character’s scope and story. If you
want to make any mechanical adjustments, such as
changing subclasses, feats, or other choices made
during the leveling process, now is the time to do so. If
you have a multiclassed character, we recommend
using this moment to re-center it into a single class,
possibly compensating for flavor losses with feats,
items, or special abilities.

If you need to create a 20th-level character from
scratch, there are two ways to do it.

1st-20th. Create a 1st-level character following the
rules of your chosen edition. Advance the character until
they reach 20th-level. Customize the character as you
see fit as if you were gaining the levels one by one.
Work with your DM to determine what additional
equipment and treasure your character might have
encountered in their adventures.

Take your time building your legend. You are not
simply making mechanical choices–each spell gained,
each feat taken, each subclass feature represents what
your character required or wished for at that moment in
their lives, what the challenges they faced drew out of
them. Think about each level, especially if you intend to
play this character throughout a somewhat long
Legendary Campaign.

We recommend not focusing too much on a preset
build you might have found on the internet that expects
a certain unruly class combination of you that only
makes sense when you are already starting at 20th
level. Multiclassing is allowed, of course, but remember,
your character would likely not have taken two levels of
fighter, three levels of rogue, and a level of barbarian
just to access a broken combo that they wouldn’t even
get to use before 18th level.

Quick Start. Use the following method to create a
quick base for your Legendary Character–this method is
best for one-off characters and playtests. The list below
is streamlined as much as possible, making it a good
way to jump directly into the Legendary Levels.

1. Choose a Class and a subclass

2. Assign the following Ability Scores: 9, 12, 13, 16, 18,

20. Choose 2 additional feats (or ability score
improvements) to round up the character’s statistics.
This creates an above-average character ready to
face the Legendary Levels.

3. Determine the Character’s hit points: Maximum

Class Hit Dice (x1) + Average Class Hit Dice (x19) +
Constitution Modifier (x20).

4. Round up your character creation by settling

customizable features such as Class options and
Spells. When writing down the plethora of abilities
your character possesses, prioritize those features
that will see more use at higher levels–does the Hide
In Plain Sight ranger feature deserve space in your
very crowded stat sheet? Probably not, but who are
we to judge?

5. With the approval of your DM, choose one Rare

Magic Item, one Very Rare Magic Item, and one
Legendary Magic Item gathered throughout your
adventures. No rings of three wishes, please.

6. Check in with your DM for any additional

adjustments, power-ups, or special features your
Legend might possess.

Original PDF page 22

Multiclassing

Multiclassed characters are a sizeable minority in nonlegendary campaigns. Each Legendary Class, however,
builds upon its existing class, meaning you need 20
levels of Barbarian to access a Barbarian’s 21st level.
To not discourage non-Legendary multiclassing in
character creation, the following rules apply.

Legendary. If your total character level is 20, you gain
the 20th-level Legendary Features of the class you
have the most levels in, but not the capstone feature
present in the non-Legendary leveling table. This means
you gain access to 1 Legendary Point, a Legendary
Point Class feature, and the Legendary Resistance
feature. A Fighter, for example, will be able to perform a
Perfect Strike and use a Legendary Resistance, but will
not have the Extra Attack (x3) feature gained at regular
20th level.

Legendary Catch-Up. Whenever you gain a Legendary
Level, you can choose to instead gain 2 levels of the
class you have the most levels in, up to 20th level.

Despite these rules, multiclassed characters will be
weaker at the Legendary Levels than their single-class
counterparts, especially if their dip into other classes is
sizeable. We recommend transforming multiclassed
characters into a single class when transitioning into a
Legendary Campaign. This supplement has a
progression system called Heroic Paths (Chapter II),
where former multiclassers can make up for lost
customization!

Legend, Paragon, Ascended

Perhaps your campaign (or one-shot!) requires
characters of a specific Legendary Level. When playing
within a given Legendary Tier, we recommend starting
at the lowest level of that tier: 20th, 22nd, or 24th. Each
jump between Legendary Tiers significantly changes
the flow of gameplay. Depending on the game you are
playing, however, the DM might ask you to jump all the
way up to 25th level!

To create a character at a specific Legendary Level,
first follow the instructions for building a 20th-level
character, then advance one level at a time alongside
your DM until you reach your desired level. Each
Legendary Level should be marked by an event—an
important milestone that propels your character into
new horizons.

Depending on the Legendary Tier, characters might
possess additional magic items, features, and powerups.

Preparing for Gameplay

A Legendary Character can do a lot of things. This can
be overwhelming, especially in character creation. For
the best experience possible, we recommend you stick
to a class you’ve played before. Though the Legendary
Levels add some interesting mechanics, they are a
direct evolution of the gameplay loop you’ve been

following since 1st level. The more familiar you are with
your character before embarking on this journey, the
better!

This is not to say you can’t experiment and throw
yourself into the depths of an entirely new class–just be
prepared to reference your non-Legendary abilities a lot!
There are many features the average D&D player has
never played with before. In the higher levels, that will
affect your gameplay a lot. Diamond Soul / Disciplined
Survivor, the Monk’s 14th-level feature, becomes an
incredibly valuable ability, especially when saving
throws determine much of what you can or cannot do
on your following turns…

DM Notes: About Player Balance
You might have heard that high-level D&D is a balancing
nightmare. Players have too many options, and stat blocks
often struggle to keep up. This book presents a selection of
capital-L Legendary Foes designed to challenge even the
best-prepared parties, while also teaching you how to use
non-legendary foes to create engaging encounters. In
addition, the systems introduced here aim to curb gamebreaking exploits and provide a framework that allows the
DM to improvise, maintain immersion, and raise the stakes
regardless of how powerful the characters become.

Perhaps your party, hardened by a years-long campaign, has
accrued a wealth of abilities, unique magic items, and storydriven boons, making them more (or in rare occasions,
less!) powerful than what is implied for non-legendary
characters created through this section. Adjusting the
party’s overall balance against the world is relatively
straightforward (see Chapter V for more information)–
always try to err on the side of the players when preparing a
character for a Legendary Campaign.

Has the rogue gained a reputation for slaying powerful
archmages over the years? Consider granting them the
Mage Slayer feat. Has the fighter earned the respect of the
dwarven clans after saving them from a hoarding dragon?
Reward them with a powerful magic weapon. Has the
sorcerer reached the pinnacle of their power, restoring an
ancient bloodline? Allow them a unique, story-driven
magical ability. These small adjustments won’t disrupt the
overall flow of the game, but they can mean the world to
your players.

What requires careful attention, however, is the perceived
balance within the party itself. This is already important in
non-legendary play—few players enjoy being consistently
overshadowed by a rogue with exceptional ability scores and
a trove of magic items. At Legendary levels, this concern
becomes even more critical. While the party as a whole may
exceed or fall short of the expected power curve presented
in this book, each character should feel equally impactful
within the group, if not in every encounter or session, at
least overall through the campaign.

Original PDF page 23

Becoming Legendary

There are three aspects of play that you must consider
when getting ready to embody a Legendary Character.

The World

What does it mean to be a Legend? At its core, the only
fundamental behind such characters is their power
relative to your regular 5e or 5.5e campaign. Though
we’ve talked about tales and myths, about gathering
magical artifacts and reaching heights yet unknown, a
Legendary Character can be whatever you, or your
world, decides.

Perhaps your campaign begins in an interstellar
prison designed to keep even Ascended-level threats
under strict surveillance–your Legend starts with the
clothes on their back, and nothing more. Perhaps the
world you inhabit is filled to the brim with powerful
creatures, and though you are considerably powerful,
there are legions composed entirely of 20th-level
warriors across the cosmos, making you a small fish in
the grand scheme. Or perhaps you are a living myth, the
last of a long-lost race of demigods, only challenged by
the greatest forces the universe has to offer. The scope
of the story will determine a lot of what being
Legendary actually is.

In most cases, you can expect a balance between
these extremes; a setting similar to that of a comic book
or an anime, in which power levels continue to rise
beyond what D&D has to offer without it seeming
contrived. Know the world your Legend inhabits and
respect it–just because you are at 20th level doesn’t
mean your character is exempt from logic.

Breaking The Game. Running a Legendary Campaign
is a hard thing, and your DM may not be as experienced
with high-level D&D as you are. You might have seen
broken combos on the internet and are dying to try them
out, such as an infinite army of constructs created over
the course of a year, a glyph of warding nuclear bomb,
or even a magic item interaction that can instantly
delete an enemy–and this is before accounting for the
Legendary rules introduced in this expansion.

Chances are, your DM will, at some point, create a
broken interaction that is clearly not working as
intended. Let them know, and don’t try to use what are
clear oversights to beat the game. The DM is trying to
create a fun experience for your table–using exploits to
push the game past its limits makes the DM’s job
harder, and ultimately makes the table less enjoyable
for everyone.

This is not to say you should avoid doing legendary
stuff, but with great power comes great responsibility. It
is on you to communicate your intentions clearly and
work with your DM, not behind their back. When you
notice something that might be problematic, find a
moment to tell them. Together, you might find that it
can be left in the game without much issue! Most of the
time, bringing it to your DM’s attention is enough for
them to plan around it.

Nazareno (playing as Astrid the Wizard): Duke Placeholderton is
clearly corrupt. I’d like to cast scrying on him, wait for him to be
alone, then teleport into his chambers, capture him in a
demiplane, replace him with a simulacrum, and have the clone
sign the treaty.

DM: Astrid certainly has the power to do that. But… the duke’s
manor sits in the middle of a city of 40 thousand people who
know and love him. Word will travel fast when their lord starts
acting strange.

Nazareno: Fair points. Astrid thinks for a moment–she’s lived
long enough to know that the quick solution might not be the
best. She requests an audience with the Duke’s assistant.

DM: Now that’s interesting. The duke receives you personally.
He’s heard of Astrid the Planeswalker. He looks cautious.
Scared, perhaps.

Manuel (playing as Manhuman the Fighter): Manhuman stands
by the door with his arms crossed. He’s not saying anything. He
just stands there, menacingly.

DM: The duke keeps glancing at Manhuman. He might sign the
treaty after all…

Manuel: Good.

DM. …but not before raising an objection with you. “See, there’s
this Lich by the name of Xerias” he begins. It seems he’s being
intimidated by this Dark Lord, who does not want this treaty to
take effect.

Nazareno. Finally! A clue! “We’re hunting this Lich, Grand Duke.
Tell us what you know. We’ll guarantee your safety.”

Manuel: Manhuman nods. It’s a powerful nod.

Original PDF page 24

The Roleplay

A Legendary Character is not simply a more powerful
version of who they were at 5th level. Years of
adventure, loss, triumph, and transformation have
shaped them into something categorically different. The
way your character carries themselves, the way they
speak to kings and Gods, the poise they have for
decisions that would paralyze a lesser hero–these are
the factors of Legendary roleplay.

This does not mean your character has to be stoic,
infallible, or above it all. Legends are defined as much
by their flaws and obsessions as by their virtues. A 20thlevel barbarian who still flies into a rage when someone
insults their homeland is not playing their character
wrong! Power does not erase who a person is. If
anything, it amplifies it.

What does change is scale. The problems a
Legendary Character concerns itself with are not the
same problems that occupied them at earlier levels. A
dispute between two merchants is beneath their notice,
unless they have a weird thing for the fairness of
bartering. The collapse of a planar boundary, the rise of
a Threadbearer, the fate of an entire civilization – these
are the stakes that feel appropriate. Let that inform how
your character moves through the world, what they
choose to engage with, and what they leave to others.

Of course, you can always subvert expectations; your
extremely powerful fighter might be obsessed with
helping out every grandma cross the street, your wizard
could be unable to ignore a certain monster infestation,
your cleric may stop at every church the party comes
across–just make sure these are quick additions that
don’t deter from the main story being told (which most
likely does concern itself with the fate of the cosmos!).

Power. Consider your character’s relationship with
their own power. Some Legends wear it lightly,
treating godlike capability as simply what they are.
Others carry it as a burden, aware of what they could
do and careful about it. Talk to your DM and your fellow
players about what kind of story you want to tell at this
tier. A Legendary campaign works best when everyone
at the table understands the genre they’re in.

Morality. The relativity of morals is a theme
exacerbated by the might of legends–if law cannot hold
you accountable, what rules will you heed?

If you choose to follow the tenets of your society, you
may become the greatest defender of their traditions
and the maker of new ones. If you perform acts of good,
your sheer power could inspire thousands to behave
nobly. If, on the other hand, you lean more into the
villainous side, you could be hunted down by
adventuring parties looking to end your rampage of
terror. Your actions are informed by a sense of right and
wrong; that balance has an oversized effect upon the
world, owing to your influence as a living myth.

Side Quests. Legendary Characters can treat most
non-Legendary adventures as a simple 5-minute ordeal.

Teleport in, slay the bad guy in one surprise round, and
return to the safety of your fort. These are quick side
quests that can add a lot of flavor to your roleplay.

John (playing as Elizabeth the Cleric): While we’re staying in
this city, can I stop by the churches for a few hours every
day and heal the sick?

DM: Of course! Your Divine Touch can lift curses, heal
wounds, and even regenerate missing limbs. The people
adore you, clamoring to have their young ones blessed by
your touch, even if nothing’s wrong with them. You begin to
gather somewhat of a cult.

John. Oh, Elizabeth doesn’t like that one bit. If the crowds
ever get too overwhelming, I’ll use my Proclamation to calm
and disperse them.

DM. You speak a divine word, and the clouds in the sky part
to let the sun illuminate you as you pass.
People move with your step, in awe of
your divine gift. They won’t bother you,
but they might erect a statue of you…

John. Elizabeth gives them a thumbs
up as she heads back into the noble
district!

Original PDF page 25

The Gameplay

As mentioned previously, the book presents five new
levels beyond the 20th for each class, along with some
general adjustments to level 20. As you level up, you will
gain access to additional class features that build upon
those you already know, while introducing three entirely
new additions to the game: Heroic Paths, Mythical
Techniques, and 10th-level Spells. These interact with
the Legendary Point System detailed in the
introduction.

Outside of legendary additions, characters keep
increasing their hit points as usual, and increase their
proficiency bonus by 1 at levels 22 and 24, when
changing Tiers of Play. Other minor improvements,
such as additional spell slots, increased rage or sneak
attack damage, and dice improvements, are detailed in
each class’s legendary leveling table.

Legendary Points. Legendary Points are your primary
resource at this tier of play, fueling your most powerful
abilities and your defenses alike. Managing them well is
what separates a character who thrives in these battles
from one who is overwhelmed by them.

Heroic Paths. With each Legendary Level, you will
gain a Heroic Perk, a point you can use to advance a
Heroic Path of your choosing. Each Heroic Path has its
own prerequisites, and some of them connect, allowing
you to create a unique legend. Think of Heroic Paths as
a continuation of your subclass, along with a
progression system similar to Epic Boons. Each step of
your Heroic Path will unlock several features, much like
a skill tree, and you can mix and match perks from
different paths to discover your very own character
combos.

You can find Heroic Paths detailed in Chapter II of this
book.

Mythical Techniques. Martials and Half-Casters will
gain access to Mythical Techniques. These are powerful
actions of varying cost that allow for incredible feats of
strength. Some may be passive abilities, while others
might require an expenditure of Legendary Points, and
others still can only be used once per day.

At different Legendary Levels, depending on your
class, you will be able to choose a Mythical Technique.
These abilities are separated by Mythical Schools, much
like spells, and are tailored to specific classes.

You can find Mythical Techniques detailed in Chapter
III of this book.

Epic Magic. Wish is no longer the mightiest spell a
mortal can cast. There are others. 10th-level spells are
the greatest effects both Characters and Foes can
create during gameplay. They require a great
investment of Legendary Points, a 10th-level spell slot,
and the right circumstances to capitalize on them. They
are a party’s greatest strength, and only come into play
at the Paragon-tier of play, meaning the jump from level

21 to level 22 is perhaps the biggest single boost of
power you will receive in your Legendary Campaign.

You can find Epic Magic detailed in Chapter IV of this
book.

Relics. Magic items change how some characters play,
making up for key weaknesses or improving their most
useful capabilities. A +3 weapon is almost mandatory in
this tier of play, along with utility objects that may help
position the party during battle. There is, however,
treasure greater even than the mighty Artifacts; Relics,
world-changing items that are connected to the
incredible foes presented in this book and their
storylines. Because of their sheer power, Relics take up
2 attunement slots instead of one.

Wielding a Relic is no easy task, with many previous
requirements and a long attunement process. Once a
character has successfully bonded with a Relic, they
increase in power substantially, some even granting
special Legendary Levels.

You can find Relics detailed in Chapter VII of this book.

Mercedes (playing as Ellie the Warlock). How many Legendary
Points does he have left after that, DM?

DM: The Hermit used 1 LP to hurl a star at you during his
turn, and he just used 2 LP to cast Prismatic Wall, so he’s
fairly spent. Still has 2 LP for the rest of the round.

Mercedes: Alright, I’ll use an LP to fire a Legendary Blast at
him and take cover behind the floating ruin there. That’s..
140 points of force damage, DM!

DM: Very well. Onto your turn, Alvar. What does Alesthesian
do?

Alvar (playing as Alesthesian the Druid). I’ll fly toward Rekhar
in my Solar form and throw him towards the hermit.

DM: And Rekhar is holding that Divine Severance for 2 LP,
right?

Hans (playing as Rehkar the Monk): Oh yeah.

DM: I see; good! Alesthesian flies 150 feet up and throws
you towards the Hermit. You loosen your mythical
technique, and a crimson burst of power escapes your
hands and becomes a wound of unendurable energy. The
Hermit rolls the save… and fails. It will use 1 LP to succeed
on that with a Legendary Resistance, though he still takes
half damage. His protective barrier is gone.

Mercedes: OK, no more Legendary Resistance for the round!

Julian (playing as Mazdamundi the Sorcerer). And no barrier
either. It’s time. I’ll use 2 LP to cast my 10th-level spell,
Theophrastus’ Flux. I’ll say, “Even stars can turn to dust” as
the transmutation burst escapes my fingers!

Original PDF page 26

Legendary Classes

For those adventurers ready to break the limits of their
mortal existence, there is a path beyond, awaiting only
the worthy. Legendary Levels are mythological–
sometimes an undiscovered height, sometimes a thing
of old powers re-ignited. Twelve classes can be found in
this section, increasing the upper limit of what each
archetype can do and creating a greater sense of power
within your world. It may take a character years,
decades, or even centuries to gather the knowledge and
practice to make such a jump. Each increase in power
beyond 20th-level is a breakthrough and may very well
be the upper limit of a character’s potential. The
difference between 20th and 25th level is magnitudes
more substantial than that between 15th and 20th level.

Level 20+

Our additions to 20th level warrant a particular
mention. When beginning a Legendary Campaign, the
first “level up” should consist of the new features of
20th level. Legendary Points, Legendary Resistance,
and Legendary Point Class Features are additions that
can entertain a party for many sessions, especially if the
group has been playing at 20th level for a while already.
21st level can wait–the players should become very
familiar with the core elements of the Legendary Point
System before advancing further.

For those campaigns that do not expect to reach
higher Legendary Tiers of Play, level 20+ can be the
power-up your party needs to face much greater foes
than before. The addition of the Legendary Resistance
feature is key in making 20th-level combat feel more
dynamic, allowing the DM to not tip-toe around the
many save-or-die spells and mechanics of high level.

“I often think of my mother. Her fate.
I think of how powerless she must
have felt. When I gained the power
to enact change, I tried to find
her soul, among the dark reaches
of Pandemonium. Although I
carried the power of Gods,
the very essence of Curiosity
and Fate, I never found her.
Even now, though I am mighty,
I am far from omnipotent.”

– Ariadne Stringweaver

Original PDF page 27

Legendary Warriors
Legendary Casters

There is more to battle than the peerless skill of a
rogue, the ancestral rage of a barbarian, or even the
divine prowess of a paladin. To go beyond 20th level as
a warrior is to harness the hidden potential of the soul,
to enhance the body beyond what seems possible, and
to perform mythical techniques only seen in tall tales.

For the purposes of this supplement, martial classes
(Barbarian, Fighter, Monk, and Rogue) and half-casters
(Paladin and Ranger) are considered warriors. To
become Legendary as one of these classes is to ascend,
to leave your imprint in history; as with spellcasters, it
means becoming something more than just a mere
mortal. Warriors in particular hone their bodies and
skills to a point that escapes conventional logic and
defies the very laws of physics, as they connect to the
true, unbound potential of the soul. A fighter who can
cut through adamantine gates is channeling a lot more
than a good slashing technique, after all.
Of Mythical Techniques

Warriors beyond 20th level gain access to the mighty
Mythical Techniques of their predecessors, a slew of
different abilities and features that have been passed
from grandmaster to grandmaster across the ages,

 dating back to the time of the Gods.

 These techniques often require absolute discipline,

 single-minded determination, and above all, a

 resolve to uncover the secrets and hardships of
 the warriors that came before. All this to have
 even the slightest chance of successfully
 performing one.
 At 25th level, Warriors gain access to several
Mythical Techniques, depending on their class.
Mythical Techniques are extremely powerful, but are
not as world-shattering as the Legendary
Spellcaster’s 10th-level spells. To balance this, Warriors
gain more power from their class features and feats,
and generally have more options to resist or outright
become immune to vast arrays of magical effects.

Mythical Techniques are the cornerstone of any
Legendary Warrior, and are almost as modular as
Heroic Paths. Depending on your choices, Mythical
Techniques will determine your character’s
effectiveness in one situation or another. While many
Mythical Techniques are actions that can be performed,
others are infusions or passive effects that activate
under specific conditions, changing the tide of battle.

The arcane weave that permeates reality is the pathway
to ultimate power, and for mortals, a means to reach the
full potential of their existence, resembling the Gods of
old in the scale of the effects they can manifest.
Surpassing 20th-level as a spellcaster implies the
acquisition of a deeper understanding of your own
magic. You are no longer an exceptionally good user;
you’re a creator. You understand each and every piece
that makes your spells work and can thus make
modifications to amplify them to their utmost potential.

Sometimes, this comes at a mental and physical cost.
Ability does not imply wisdom in its application, and
many mages have been felled by excessive prana
channeling…

Of Epic Magic

The most impressive power of legendary spellcasters is
their ability to cast spells of 10th level, the mightiest
arcanisms mortals can channel. At 25th level, full
casters have access to two 10th-level spells, though they
can only cast one 10th-level spell between long rests.
Learning 10th-level spells is quite an ordeal; a caster
can only learn a 10th-level spell when it is noted in their
class descriptions.

The use of a 10th-level spell is an event of mythic
proportions, often directly tied to the conclusion of
pivotal battles, the fall of legendary leaders, or the end
of an era and beginning of another. Thus, a character
that learns a 10th-level spell is leaving an imprint in
history. We provide many 10th-level spells in this
supplement that you can drag and drop into your game,
but we deeply encourage you to make your own when a
player character is involved! This is their ultimate
power, the sum of their arcane ability and personal
style. It should be unique to them. As you will see, many
10th-level spells are named after the illustrious
individuals from our universe who invented them.

Original PDF page 28

Legendary Barbarian

The old stories speak of warriors who could stop an
army with a roar, whose fury summoned storms, whose
blood ran so hot that it melted the steel that dared cut
them. Those stories have a true, brutal basis in the
Legendary Barbarians of the past… and the present.

A Legendary Barbarian is rage given form, purpose,
and permanence. Unlike the fleeting anger of a soldier
in the heat of battle, their rage has been burning since
the first time the world tried to break them–to no avail.
Every scar they carry is a mark of their endurance, every
enemy defeated another verse of a saga written in ash
and blood.

Few forces in the cosmos are as terrifying or as
straightforward as a Legendary Barbarian.
Where others have spent their
adventuring careers accumulating
spells, sophisticated maneuvers,
and arcane contingencies, the
Barbarian has channeled their
potential into a body that can weather
any blow, and answer in kind. At 20th
level, that dedication
has compounded into
something that defies the logic of
the physical world. A mortal is not
supposed to withstand the forces
that routinely assail a Legendary
Barbarian, nor be able to summon the
overwhelming strength that permeates
each of their strikes. They thunder forward
on the battlefield, walking catastrophes whose
path you must part from, or be torn apart yourself.
To mighty immortals used to crudely asserting their
physical dominance over meek mortals, they are the
ultimate blight. To their legendary companions, they are
an unfailing assurance: even if they should all fall, the
living tempest by their side will fall last, and ensure the
enemy pays dearly for their victory.
Original PDF page 29

Level 20

Legendary Points

Starting at 20th level, you gain Legendary Points. Your
level determines the number of points you have, as
shown in the Legendary Points column of the
Legendary Barbarian table. You can take the Recover
action on your turn to regain all expended Legendary
Points. You can spend these points to fuel various
features.

Legendary Resistance

Starting at 20th level, you can expend 1 Legendary
Point when you fail a saving throw to succeed instead.

Devastating Strike

When you reach 20th level, you learn to channel your
immense strength into a singular, devastating strike.
You can use your action and expend 1 Legendary Point
to make a melee weapon attack roll against a target
within range. On a hit, you deal 5d12 x 5 damage of the
weapon’s type. This attack does not benefit from any
bonus to its damage. On a critical hit, you do not
multiply the damage dice by 2, but instead deal 7d12 x 5
weapon damage.

A creature hit by Devastating Strike must succeed on
a Constitution saving throw against your Strength score
or become Impaired for a minute.

5.5e: If you use Reckless Attack on your Devastating
Strike, you can forgo any Advantage on the attack roll.
The Multiplier Attack must not have Disadvantage. If
the attack hits, you add 1 die to the Devastating Strike’s
damage equation, and you can cause two Brutal Strike
effects of your choice. If the attack misses, you can

Points
Rages
Rage
Damage

Weapon
Mastery

expend 1 use of your Rage to reroll the attack. You must
use the new roll.

Legendary AC

At 20th level, if your AC calculation is lower than your
level, you can use your level as your AC instead. If you
are using your Legendary AC, you cannot further
increase your AC in any way, except by temporary AC
increases or magical effects lasting 1 hour or less. Other
spells and effects that increase your AC only affect your
regular AC.

Level 21

Heroic Path Advancement

At 21st level, you choose a Heroic Path you meet the
prerequisites for and advance its first step. Each time
you gain a level, you can advance a Heroic Path by one
step, start a new Heroic Path, or, if specified as an
option of the current step you’re on, jump from one
Heroic Path into another.

Legendary Score Improvement

When you reach 21st level, and again at levels 23 and
25, increase one of your ability scores by 2 or two ability
scores by 1. You cannot raise an ability score above 24
using this feature, unless another feature raised your
maximum for that specific ability score beforehand.

Primordial Champion

Also at 21st level, your maximum for your Strength
and Constitution scores becomes 30.

Original PDF page 31

Mythical Technique

At 21st level, you learn a Mythical Technique from the
Mythical Techniques section. You learn an additional
Mythical Technique when you reach 24th level.

5.5e: Whenever you learn a Mythical Technique, you
gain an additional Weapon Mastery.

Level 22

Bulwark of Constitution

Beginning at 22nd level, increase your Constitution
score by 2. You increase your Constitution score by an
additional 2 when you reach 24th level.

Barbaric Leap

Also at 22nd level, you learn to use your strength to your
benefit when crossing large distances. You leap into the
skies, descending onto a point you can see within 500
feet of you. All creatures within 20 feet of that point
when you land must succeed on a Dexterity saving
throw against your Strength score, taking 45 (10d8)
bludgeoning damage and being knocked prone on a
failed save, or taking half as much damage and not
being knocked prone on a successful one. When using
this feature, you don’t take fall damage when falling 500
feet or less.

LP Cost. You can use this feature as a bonus action on
your turn. If you do so, you expend 1 Legendary Point.

Legendary Rage

Whenever you enter a rage, you can expend 1
Legendary Point to gain 100 temporary hit points that
last for a minute. Once you use this feature, you cannot
do so again until you finish a long rest.

5.5e: When using your Legendary Rage, you can
expend additional Rage uses to increase the temporary
hit points you gain. For each Rage use you expend, you
gain an additional 20 temporary hit points, up to a
maximum of 100. This cap is removed when you gain
the Improved Legendary Rage feature at 25th level.

Also, when you reach 22nd level, and again at 24th
level, you gain an additional use of your Rage feature.

Level 23

Challenging Roar

Starting at 23rd level, your challenging presence is
impossible to ignore, even for legends. As a bonus
action, you can unleash a primal roar. Each creature of
your choice within 30 feet must succeed on a Wisdom
saving throw against a DC equal to your Strength score
or be frightened of you for 1 minute. A frightened
creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each
of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.

Creatures immune to the frightened condition must
make this saving throw regardless. On a failure, the

creature is beckoned by your challenge and compelled
to attack you until the end of its next turn. While
compelled in this way, a creature cannot make attack
rolls against a creature other than you if you are within
range and a valid target. If the creature cannot reach
you with its attacks or is otherwise unable to damage
you, it may target another creature instead.

LP Cost. You can use this feature at the end of another
creature’s turn by expending 1 Legendary Point.

Crushing Critical

Also at 23rd level, your critical hits can put even the
greatest foes out of commission. When you score a
critical hit, the target must succeed on a Constitution
saving throw against your Strength score. On a failure,
they are stunned until the end of their next turn. If the
target is immune to the Stunned condition, it is
Impaired instead for the same duration.

Level 24

Effortless Resistance

Beginning at 24th level, the first time you use your
Legendary Resistance feature in between long rests,
you do not expend a Legendary Point. Additionally, you
can use the Legendary Resistance feature any number
of times in a round, as long as you have the Legendary
Points to fuel them.

Improved Devastating Strike

When you reach 24th level, your Devastating Strike
damage calculation is increased to 8d12 x 5, or 10d12 x
5 on a critical hit.

Level 25

Aura of Endurance

Starting at 25th level, you gain the ability to extend your
endurance to all who march into battle with you. As a
bonus action, you can expend 1 Legendary Point to
create an Aura of Endurance that lasts for a minute.
Choose a damage type from the following: acid, cold,
fire, lightning, or thunder. You and all allied creatures
within 15 feet of you cannot be critically hit, become
resistant to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage,
and are immune to the chosen damage type for the
duration. You can only choose one damage type at a
time. If you want to change the chosen damage type,
you must expend a Legendary Point and use a bonus
action on your turn.

Improved Legendary Rage

Also at 25th level, you can expend any number of
Legendary Points when you use your Legendary Rage
feature. For each LP expended, you gain an additional
100 temporary hit points.

Original PDF page 32

Legendary Bard

The greatest Bards who ever lived did not die in battle,
but in a theater’s stage, mid-performance, at the ripe old
age of two hundred and twelve–and the audience wept
for days. They died in their last epic adventure, ensuring
their place in the annals of history before turning to
golden dust. They died taking the secrets of the
Universe to the grave, after decades of using them to
shape our understanding of existence in service of their
goals. Their songs outlasted the kingdoms they exalted.
Their name survived long after none could speak the
language that spawned it. Somewhere, on a plane most
mortals will never visit, someone is still humming their
melodies, unaware of the power behind each word
composed in an Age past.

That is what a Legendary Bard is. A phenomenon.
The kind of figure that civilizations build statues to,
religions quietly absorb, and tyrants try to silence or
desperately attempt to hire. A Legendary Bard
commands the interest even of Gods, because they
understand something most do not: a story well told is
more durable than anything. Empires may have
fractured, and adventurer-kings lost their power. Yet
their stories remain. Who sang those songs? Who wrote
those epics? Someone who knew that, by definition, a
legend is not a person, but a tale.

At 20th level, the Bard’s performances reshape the
emotional truth of a moment, bending the will of kings,
steadying the nerves of armies, and unraveling the
certainty of enemies far superior. There are planar lords
who have ruled for millennia and never commanded a
room the way a Legendary Bard can.

It may take a magma-blooded barbarian to lift a
mountain, but it takes a bard to make anyone else
believe it ever happened–even if it didn’t happen. After
all, what is a tale without its teller?
Original PDF page 33

Level 20
Legendary Inspiration

Legendary Points

Starting at 20th level, you gain Legendary Points. Your
level determines the number of points you have, as
shown in the Legendary Points column of the
Legendary Bard table. You can take the Recover action
on your turn to regain all expended Legendary Points.
You can spend these points to fuel various features.

Legendary Resistance

Starting at 20th level, you can expend 1 Legendary
Point when you fail a saving throw to succeed instead.

Legendary AC

At 20th level, if your AC calculation is lower than your
level, you can use your level as your AC instead. If you
are using your Legendary AC, you cannot further
increase your AC in any way, except by temporary AC
increases or magical effects lasting 1 hour or less. Other
spells and effects that increase your AC only affect your
regular AC.

Cantrips

Prepared

Known

Spells

Also at 20th level, you gain the ability to inspire legends.
As a bonus action on your turn, you can expend 1
Legendary Point and a use of your Bardic Inspiration to
inspire a creature within 120 feet. The creature regains
1 Legendary Point and becomes inspired as per your
Bardic Inspiration feature.

Level 21

Heroic Path Advancement

At 21st level, you choose a Heroic Path you meet the
prerequisites for and advance its first step. Each time
you gain a level, you can advance a Heroic Path by one
step, start a new Heroic Path, or, if specified as an
option of the current step you’re on, jump from one
Heroic Path into another.

Original PDF page 35

Legendary Score Improvement

When you reach 21st level, and again at levels 23 and
25, increase one of your ability scores by 2 or two ability
scores by 1. You cannot raise an ability score above 24
using this feature, unless another feature raised your
maximum for that specific ability score beforehand.

Note of Rebuttal

Also at 21st level, your knowledge of the arcane has
given you considerable insight into how to control the
enemy. When a creature within 60 feet of you casts a
spell or makes an attack, you can use your reaction and
expend 1 Legendary Point to perform a Note of
Rebuttal. If the creature is casting a spell, you cast
Counterspell at 7th level without expending a spell slot.
If the creature is making an attack, you speak a word of
power that overwhelms the mind, interrupting the
attack.

Level 22

10th-Level Spell

At 22nd level, you learn a single 10th-level spell. You
learn an additional 10th-level spell at 24th level. When
you start casting a 10th-level spell, you must expend 2
Legendary Points alongside the required action and
spell slot.

Twinned Inspiration

Starting at 22nd level, your inspiration has become even
stronger. When you use your Bardic Inspiration, you
can inspire one additional creature within range. In
addition, when you use your Legendary Inspiration, you
can choose to expend an extra Legendary Point to
target one additional creature.

Level 23

Masterful Inspiration

Beginning at 23rd level, your Bardic Die becomes a
d20. In addition, whenever you use your Bardic
Inspiration, you can cast a spell of 5th level or lower
with a casting time of one action or bonus action as part
of the same bonus action.

Song of Legend

Also at 23rd level, you learn to give a legendary
performance that enhances your allies and intimidates
your enemies. As an action on your turn, you can play
one of the following powerful melodies:

+

Respite: You and up to 10 allies who can hear you
within 60 feet gain an amount of temporary hit
points equal to five times your Charisma modifier.

+

Tragedy: Creatures of your choice that can hear you
within 60 feet must succeed on a Wisdom saving
throw or become frightened of you until the end of
their next turn. A creature that is immune to the
frightened condition is Impaired for a minute
instead.

+

Valor: You and allies that can hear you within 60
feet gain a +3 to attack and damage rolls until the
end of your next turn.

Level 24

Symphony of Arcana

Beginning at 24th level, your knowledge of the world
and the secrets of magic exceeds even that of wizards.
You learn four spells of 8th level or lower from any
class, as per your Magical Secrets feature.

Inspirational Recovery

Also at 24th level, when you use the Recover action, you
regain 1d4 + 1 uses of your Bardic Inspiration feature.

Level 25

Hymn of Legend

Starting at 25th level, your Song of Legend becomes
more powerful.

+

Recovery: When you use the Respite option, you and
up to 4 creatures within range regain 1 Legendary
Point.

+

Catastrophe: When you use the Tragedy option,
creatures of your choice within the area take an
amount of psychic damage equal to five times your
Charisma modifier and must succeed on a
Constitution saving throw against your spell save
DC. On a failure, they are stunned until the end of
their next turn.

+

Zeal: When you use the Valor option, you can
expend 1 Legendary Point to give you and up to 4
creatures within range that can hear you an
additional action on their next turn. That action can
be used only to take the Attack (one weapon attack
only), Dash, Disengage, Hide, or Use an Object
actions.

Original PDF page 36

Legendary Cleric

Clerics are known far and wide as vessels of a higher
power, chosen ones of varying might who speak with a
voice and authority that is not their own–much as they
might agree with it. Where Legendary Clerics are
concerned, that line begins to blur until it disappears
entirely. Their capacity of channeling a Divine Domain
is such that they come to incarnate it, partially or wholly
becoming the deities they once looked up to in devotion
and prayer. One must accept that the right hand of the
God of Light, for example, is an extension of the God of
Light. At the highest echelon of achievement, devotion
begets fusion with its object, and ultimately…
apotheosis.

A Legendary Cleric may refuse this interpretation of
their newfound power, instead pointing to extraordinary
divine favor granted for equally extraordinary devotion.
The fact remains that, at this tier, their faith has become
indistinguishable from divine authority. Beyond 20th
level, a Cleric does not petition; they speak directly on
behalf of Divinity. Their magic is rooted in declarations,
issued by someone who has spent a lifetime becoming
the closest thing to a God that a mortal can manage
without ceasing to be a mortal. Armies halt at their
word. The dead return at their beckoning. The planes
themselves respond to their sacred presence.

In the midst of other Legendary Characters, a
Legendary Cleric stands out as an unparalleled beacon
of legitimacy. Divinity, at its core, is the foundation of
the Universe, and those who incarnate it are its
architects and keepers, be they Gods… or clerics.
Original PDF page 37

Level 20

Legendary Points

Starting at 20th level, you gain Legendary Points. Your
level determines the number of points you have, as shown
in the Legendary Points column of the Legendary Cleric
table. You can take the Recover action on your turn to
regain all expended Legendary Points. You can spend
these points to fuel various features.

Legendary Resistance

Starting at 20th level, you can expend 1 Legendary Point
when you fail a saving throw to succeed instead.

Legendary AC

At 20th level, if your AC calculation is lower than your
level, you can use your level as your AC instead. If you are
using your Legendary AC, you cannot further increase
your AC in any way, except by temporary AC increases or

Cantrips

magical effects lasting 1 hour or less. Other spells and
effects that increase your AC only affect your regular AC.

Burst of Divinity

Also at 20th level, as an action, you can expend 1
Legendary Point and point your holy symbol at a creature
you can see within 60 feet. You either restore hit points to
the creature equal to your level x 5 or have the creature
take an amount of Necrotic or Radiant damage (your
choice) equal to your level x 5.

Level 21

Heroic Path Advancement

At 21st level, you choose a Heroic Path you meet the
prerequisites for and advance its first step. Each time you
gain a level, you can advance a Heroic Path by one step,
start a new Heroic Path, or, if specified as an option of the
current step you’re on, jump from one Heroic Path into
another.

Original PDF page 39

Legendary Score Improvement

When you reach 21st level, and again at levels 23 and
25, increase one of your ability scores by 2 or two ability
scores by 1. You cannot raise an ability score above 24
using this feature, unless another feature raised your
maximum for that specific ability score beforehand.

Warded by a Higher Power

Also at 21st level, your God ensures your protection if
you are ever in true danger. Whenever you take damage
or are subjected to an effect that would reduce you to 0
hit points or kill you outright, you can use your reaction
to regain 50 hit points and become immune to all
damage for one minute. This protection affects the
triggering damage. If you make an attack, use an object,
or cast a spell, this protection ends.

Once you use this feature, you must finish a long rest
before you can use it again.

Level 22

10th-Level Spell

At 22nd level, you learn a single 10th-level spell. You
learn an additional 10th-level spell at 24th level. When
you start casting a 10th-level spell, you must expend 2
Legendary Points alongside the required action and
spell slot.

Divine Mandate

Also at 22nd level, the sacred ordainment that guides
you becomes powerful enough to momentarily warp
reality to your God’s will, needing only the right words.
As an action on your turn, you can cause one of the
following effects. You must be able to speak to use this
ability.

To Dust Commend Thy Walls. Choose a creature you
can see within 60 feet, and select either two damage
types it is resistant to, or one damage type it is immune
to. It loses those resistances or that immunity for the
next minute.

Weight Be Upon The Sinful. Creatures of your choice
within 60 feet must succeed on a Strength saving throw
against your spell save DC or be knocked prone and be
incapacitated until the start of your next turn. Creatures
whose alignment is opposite to yours in any axis roll
this saving throw with disadvantage.

Dauntless Are The Virtuous. Creatures of your
choice within 60 feet roll all ability checks, attack rolls,
contests, and saving throws with advantage until the
start of your next turn.

Level 23

Heraldic Favor

Beginning at 23rd level, you become the foremost
herald of your God. Your voice is theirs, and you can

perform miracles in their name. After successfully
performing a Divine Intervention, you can do so again
after you complete a long rest, instead of having to wait
7 days. In addition, your Divine Intervention becomes
even more powerful, able to replicate any spell of 9th
level or lower from the Cleric spell list.

Level 24

Fountain of Grace

Beginning at 24th level, your holy presence constantly
empowers you and your allies. Creatures of your choice
within 30 feet of you have a +2 bonus to ability checks,
attack rolls, contests, and saving throws.

Blessed Recovery

Also at 24th level, when you use the Recover action, you
regain all uses of your Channel Divinity feature.

Level 25

Divine Indemnity

At 25th level, your God grants you control over the
powers of protection they have bestowed upon your
blessed self.

You can expend 2 Legendary Points and use your
action to give a creature within 60 feet of you immunity
to all damage for up to 1 minute. You can only protect
one creature in this manner at a time. The protection
breaks if the target creature is ever farther than 500 feet
away from you or on a different plane of existence. This
counts as a magical effect of 9th level for the purposes
of Tiers of Magic and uses your concentration.

Original PDF page 40

Legendary Druid

Even the most bull-headed industrialist will admit that,
given the smallest foothold, nature thrives and expands
in ways that few other powers in existence can match.
And, like mortal armies have their fighters of myth,
religions their radiant paladins, and interplanar crime
syndicates their unseen rogues, nature finds its greatest
exponents in Legendary Druids. Their attunement to
the mysterious flow of the natural world is such that
their wisdom applies to everything, not just the
wilderness they hail from. In the end, every single
thing–from the clothes on the backs of mortals and
immortals alike, to the food they eat, to even the
weapons they wield–comes from their domain.

A Legendary Druid is the ultimate protector of that
which comes from nature’s gift. They have spent their
life tending what began as seeds and clumps of
cells, learning its rhythms, earning its trust, becoming
fluent in a language older than most Gods. They have
heard things that would shatter a lesser mind–the slow
grief of a forest dwindling and dying over centuries, the
fury of a storm that remembers when the land was not
there, the vast and patient memory of a mountain that
has watched civilizations built in its shadow turn to
dust. These druids did not break under the strain of
knowledge and wisdom. They grew around it the way
ancient roots grow over stone, unhurried and quietly,
yet terrifyingly permanent.

At 20th level, the Druid is an expression of the natural
world–its voice, its memory, its oldest and most patient
form of resistance. The power they wield feels like
natural consequence. When a Legendary Druid calls
down a storm, the sky agrees with their command.
When they walk into a blighted land, the dying flowers
notice their step and grow anew–or wilt and disappear,
becoming nutrients for something new. When these
druids speak, ancient things that have remained quiet
turn to act, from rocks to roots, to great rivers and
forests. Something fundamental accompanies such a
paragon of the wild, something that carries, however
distantly, the echo of the Great Mother herself: life–
violent, unruly, overgrown life, ready to kill and die at
their behest. Those who fight next to a Legendary Druid
are accompanied by primal forces that they don’t truly
understand, but are unvaryingly thankful for.

Level 20

Legendary Points

Starting at 20th level, you gain Legendary Points. Your
level determines the number of points you have, as shown
in the Legendary Points column of the Legendary Druid
table. You can take the Recover action on your turn to
regain all expended Legendary Points. You can spend
these points to fuel various features.

Legendary Resistance

Starting at 20th level, you can expend 1 Legendary Point
when you fail a saving throw to succeed instead.

Legendary AC

At 20th level, if your AC calculation is lower than your
level, you can use your level as your AC instead. If you are
using your Legendary AC, you cannot further increase
your AC in any way, except by temporary AC increases or
magical effects lasting 1 hour or less. Other spells and
effects that increase your AC only affect your regular AC.

Cantrips

Prepared

Known

Spells

Legendary Forms

Also at 20th level, you gain the ability to transform into
greater forms as part of your wild shape. When you use
your Wild Shape, you can expend 1 Legendary Point to
turn into any elemental, fey, or monstrosity you have seen
before of a Challenge Rating equal to or lower than 10.
You transform into an average example of that creature,
one without any class levels or the Spellcasting trait. You
can’t use any legendary actions, legendary resistances, or
lair actions of the new form.

Level 21

Heroic Path Advancement

At 21st level, you choose a Heroic Path you meet the
prerequisites for and advance its first step. Each time you
gain a level, you can advance a Heroic Path by one step,
start a new Heroic Path, or, if specified as an option of the
current step you’re on, jump from one Heroic Path into
another.

Original PDF page 43

Legendary Score Improvement

When you reach 21st level, and again at levels 23 and
25, increase one of your ability scores by 2 or two ability
scores by 1. You cannot raise an ability score above 24
using this feature, unless another feature raised your
maximum for that specific ability score beforehand.

Avenger of the Wild

Also at 21st level, your attunement to the wild allows
you to call upon natural disasters. As an action, you can
expend a Wild Shape use to cast one of the following
spells once per day without expending a spell slot:
Earthquake, Incendiary Cloud, or Sunburst. Once you
use this feature to cast one of these spells, you cannot
use it to cast the same spell again until you finish a long
rest.

LP Cost. You can use this feature at the end of another
creature’s turn by expending 1 Legendary Point.

Level 22

10th-Level Spell

At 22nd level, you learn a single 10th-level spell. You
learn an additional 10th-level spell at 24th level. When
you start casting a 10th-level spell, you must expend 2
Legendary Points alongside the required action and
spell slot.

Verdant Crown

Also at 22nd level, you can use an action to don a
Verdant Crown for the next minute, a symbol of
absolute authority from your Druid Circle. The crown
can be worn while shapeshifted and counts as an 8thlevel spell for the purposes of Tiers of Magic.

While crowned in this way, you can concentrate on an
additional spell at a time. If your concentration is
broken, all spells you were concentrating on end. The
number of spells you can concentrate on at a time can
never exceed 3, even if you gain this same benefit from
various sources.

In addition, your natural glory causes Beasts and Plants
of CR 15 or lower within 120 feet of you to be
completely enthralled. They are friendly to you, can
understand you irrespective of what language you
speak, and will heed all commands issued by you,
unless they are directly harmful to them.

Improved Legendary Forms

Also at 22nd level, the Challenge Rating limit of your
Legendary Forms is increased to 15.

5.5e: You gain an additional use of your Wild Shape
feature. When you reach 24th level, you gain an
additional use.

Level 23

Extraplanar Forms

Beginning at 23rd level, you gain the ability to transform
into greater forms as part of your wild shape. When you
use your Wild Shape, you can expend 2 Legendary
Points to turn into any aberration, celestial, or fiend you
have seen before of a Challenge Rating of 19 or lower.
You transform into an average example of that creature,
one without any class levels or the Spellcasting trait.
You can’t use any legendary actions, legendary
resistances, or lair actions of the new form.

Level 24

Archdruid’s Resilience

When you reach 24th level, you become resistant to
cold and fire damage, both in your true form and in your
Wild Shape. Additionally, whenever you finish a long
rest, choose one other damage type among the
following: acid, lightning, or thunder. You become
resistant to that damage type in all your forms until you
choose another type upon finishing a long rest.

Rejuvenating Recovery

Also at 24th level, when you use the Recover action, you
regain a use of your Wild Shape feature and are
cleansed of one harmful condition or magical effect of
7th level or lower currently afflicting you.

Level 25

Final Form

At 25th level, your wild shape mastery makes you able
to replicate even the most exotic and unnatural
lifeforms. When you use your Wild Shape, you can
choose to expend 3 Legendary Points to turn into any
creature that isn’t a Construct that you have seen
before, of a Challenge Rating equal to or lower than
your Druid level. You transform into an average
example of that creature, one without any class levels or
the Spellcasting trait. You can’t use any legendary
actions, legendary resistances, or lair actions of the new
form.

5.5e: Additionally, when you convert uses of Wild
Shape into a spell slot, each use of your Wild Shape
contributes to 3 spell levels. For example, if you convert
two uses of Wild Shape, you produce a 6th-level spell
slot. You cannot use this feature to gain a 10th-level
spell slot. Once you use this benefit, you can’t do so
again until you finish a long rest.

Original PDF page 44

Legendary Fighter

The hard, practical mind of the Legendary Fighter is not
something easily bought. In battle, loss is inevitable, and
those who live for it tend to die for it sooner rather than
later. A Legendary Fighter, be it for their drive,
intelligence, talent, or sheer physical prowess, has
thrived in that hell, and remains alive against brutally
unfavorable odds–the chance of death in each crossing
of swords, multiplied by ten thousand skirmishes. Their
deeds paint their prowess as unparalleled, heroes of
legend that snatch victory from the jaws of defeat time
and time again. Yet for them, that martial capacity is but
the result of a lifetime of training, of being knocked
down, and of living to fight another day. This logic is
what made them invincible in their own art.

The cosmos is full of beings that were born to power–
Gods who emerged divine, mortals who seized the
remnants of ancients, immortal creatures who accrued
their strength across millennia of unchallenged
existence. The Fighter was not born to it, and, had
reason been followed, was destined for an
unceremonious, early death. Every ability, every reflex,
every scar was earned, one battle at a time–emerging
victorious, or at least alive, against fate itself.

That is what beats at the hearts of Legendary
Fighters, what commands their will and allows them to
lead armies, conquer worlds, and slay Gods. In the end,
no matter their accolades and storied accomplishments,
they will always be that struggling soul, heaving against
the world, crawling through the mud to get a ray of sun,
and paying for it with blood and sweat. At 20th level, the
Fighter has become the sum of every battle they have
survived, distilled into something that operates at a level
beyond technique, experience, and even logic. They act
with a commitment and a depth of wisdom that no spell,
no divine gift, and no cosmic inheritance can fully
replicate. On a battlefield of Gods and monsters, the
Legendary Fighter stands on equal ground. Their blade
against the world, and the thrill of battle coming to their
aid.

pages.name: p. 47 - Weapon Attack. Perfect Strike counts as a weapon

text.content:
Original PDF page 47

Weapon Attack. Perfect Strike counts as a weapon
attack and can be modified by or apply additional effects
such as Weapon Masteries, Maneuvers, and Mythical
Technique Infusions. Effects that add damage to a
weapon attack do not apply.

Level 21

Heroic Path Advancement

At 21st level, you choose a Heroic Path you meet the
prerequisites for and advance its first step. Each time
you gain a level, you can advance a Heroic Path by one
step, start a new Heroic Path, or, if specified as an
option of the current step you’re on, jump from one
Heroic Path into another.

Legendary Score Improvement

When you reach 21st level, and again at levels 23 and
25, increase one of your ability scores by 2 or two ability
scores by 1. You cannot raise an ability score above 24
using this feature, unless another feature raised your
maximum for that specific ability score beforehand.

Mythical Technique

At 21st level, you learn a Mythical Technique from the
Mythical Techniques section. You learn an additional
Mythical Technique when you reach 22nd, 23rd, and
24th levels.

5.5e: Whenever you learn a Mythical Technique, you
gain an additional Weapon Mastery.

Improved Second Wind

At 21st level, you gain an additional use of your Second
Wind feature. Whenever you use your Second Wind
feature to heal, you gain temporary hit points equal to
1d10 + your fighter level.

You gain an additional charge of Second Wind at 23rd
and 25th levels.

Level 22

Perfect Defense

Beginning at 22nd level, as long as you are wielding a
weapon, you reduce the damage from weapon attacks
by 10. In addition, whenever you are subject to an effect
that allows you to make a Dexterity saving throw to take
only half damage, you instead take no damage if you
succeed and only half damage if you fail.

Legendary Warrior

Starting at 22nd level, you have learned the ways of
battle and have proven yourself a legendary warrior.
You learn 2 additional fighting styles from any class.
You cannot be disarmed or forced to drop your weapon,
and you become immune to the frightened condition.

Level 23

Legendary Action

At 23rd level, you can expend 1 Legendary Point to take
an action at the end of another creature’s turn. You
cannot take the Recover action in this way. If you take
the Attack action, you can make up to 3 attacks. Once
you use your Legendary Action, you cannot do so again
until the end of your next turn.

Level 24

Masterful Strike

When you reach 24th level, your Perfect Strike dice
categories are increased. If you are using a melee
weapon, d8s are replaced by d12s. If you are using a
ranged weapon, d6s are replaced by d10s.

Powerful Reserve

Also at 24th level, you can push your limits even when
there is nothing more to give. If you have 0 Legendary
Points, you can still use a feature that costs 1 Legendary
Point. If you do so, the next time you use the Recover
action, it does not restore any Legendary Points.

Level 25

Kensei Technique

At 25th level, you learn one Kensei Technique–a more
powerful version of a Mythical Technique. Kensei
Techniques follow the same rules as Mythical
Techniques, as detailed in Part III, with one exception:
once you use a Kensei Technique, you cannot do so
again until you finish a long rest.

pages.name: p. 48 - The following techniques are available exclusively to Legendary Fighters. E

text.content:
Original PDF page 48

The following techniques are available exclusively to Legendary Fighters. Each represents the absolute pinnacle of a
particular martial school. A Fighter who learns a Kensei Technique has gone beyond what the school was designed to

teach. Kensei Techniques follow all the same rules as Mythical Techniques, but can only be used once per long rest,

Kensei Techniques

The School of Mastery

regardless of their LP cost.

Death of the Soul

Strike, Ichimonji

Classes: Fighter
Channeling Time: 1 action
Range: Weapon range, up to 60 feet
Duration: Instantaneous
LP Cost: 3 LP

You channel your soul into your weapon, releasing twin
bursts of white flame. Make two weapon attacks against
either one target or two separate targets within range.

Soul Strike. On a hit, the attack deals Soul damage
instead of its normal damage type and the target’s soul
is expelled from its body, casting it into the Ethereal
Plane. The body falls unconscious and cannot be
awakened while the soul remains separated.

The expelled soul appears within 5 feet of its body on
the Ethereal Plane, retaining the creature’s full
statistics. It can act on each of its turns but is confined
to the Ethereal Plane and can only affect creatures and
objects there. At the start of each of its turns, the soul
can attempt to return to its body by succeeding on a
Charisma saving throw against your Mythical
Technique DC. On a success, the soul returns and the
creature regains consciousness. Damage dealt to the
soul is also dealt to the body, but damage dealt to the
body does not affect the soul. When body and soul are
reunited, the creature’s hit points become whichever of
the two counts is lower. If the body is destroyed before
the soul returns, the soul is lost in the Ethereal and
cannot return unless the body is resurrected. If neither
body nor soul has been destroyed after 1 minute, the
soul snaps back automatically.

Creatures with no soul–such as certain Constructs
and Undead–are unaffected by this technique. This
technique can hit a creature in the ethereal plane.

Double Strike. If both attacks target the same creature
and both hit, that creature must make a Charisma
saving throw against your Mythical Technique DC. On a
failure, its soul is not merely expelled–it is destroyed
entirely. The creature dies instantly. Nothing short of a
Wish spell cast at 10th level can restore a soul
destroyed in this way.

Kensei’s Justice

Strike, Ichimonji

Classes: Fighter
Channeling Time: 1 action
Range: Unlimited
Duration: Concentration, up to 1 minute
LP Cost: 2 LP

You channel your soul into a powerful and inevitable
strike, marking a creature within range. For the
duration, whenever the marked target takes an action,
bonus action, or Extra Action, you can use your reaction
to teleport to an unoccupied space within 60 feet of it,
regardless of the distance between you–even across
planes of existence. When you teleport in this way, you
can make a single attack against the target with
advantage.

On a hit, the target takes an additional 110 (20d10)
Soul damage and must succeed on a Constitution
saving throw against your Mythical Technique save DC
or be stunned until the end of its next turn. If the target
is immune to the stunned condition, it is impaired
instead.

The effect ends early if the target dies.

Imprisonment. If this attack reduces the target to 0 hit
points, its soul becomes bound inside your weapon as
per the Imprisonment spell, to be released upon a
condition of your choosing.

Original PDF page 49

Rokkaku’s Sixteen Strikes

Strike, Zulfiqar

Classes: Fighter
Channeling Time: 1 action
Range: Weapon range or reach
Duration: Instantaneous
LP Cost: 2 LP

You infuse your weapon with a phantom echo of your
own fighting spirit, and in a single devastating moment,
every strike you could possibly deliver is realized at
once.

As an action, you perform up to 16 special weapon
attacks against targets within range. On a hit, each
strike deals 20 Lethal damage.

These attacks do not benefit from Mythical Technique
Infusions. On a critical hit, the damage for an attack is
not doubled, but rather increases to 25. If the creature is
bloodied and Lethal damage takes effect, the damage is
not tripled; each critical strike deals 30 damage instead.
These attacks do not benefit from any other bonus to
their damage.

You can divide these attacks freely among any
number of targets within range.

If you perform this technique with a ranged weapon,
the maximum number of attacks is reduced to 12.

Greater Lotus Barrier

Strike, Ryuushinkan

Classes: Fighter
Channeling Time: 1 reaction, which you take when
you or a creature within 30 feet of you takes damage
Range: 30 feet
Duration: 1 round
LP Cost: 2 LP

You project a barrier of pure soul-energy that
encompasses you and up to 7 allies within range,
interposing itself between them and all harm. The
barrier absorbs damage directed at any protected
creature and can withstand up to 1,000 points of
damage total before breaking. It lasts until the end of
your next turn.

For the duration, each creature protected by the
Lotus Barrier cannot be forcibly moved, grappled,
knocked prone, or displaced. They are immune to any
effect that would reduce them to 0 hit points or kill them
outright. The barrier protects against the triggering
damage, against any secondary effects that damage
would cause–such as a poison or a magical condition–
and against the effects of other Mythical Techniques,
including other Kensei Techniques.

If the barrier is destroyed, any damage in excess of
the barrier’s remaining threshold is dealt to the creature
or creatures targeted by the source that destroyed it.

Original PDF page 50

Legendary Monk

There is a story told in certain monasteries–the kind
built into cliffsides, accessible only to those who already
know the way–about a student who wished to stop the
rain. Though his fellows laughed at his goal, he stood
beneath pouring drops for days and days, attempting to
punch, kick, or otherwise detain something that seemed
unstoppable. His master, moved by the student’s
dedication, walked behind him, into the rain, and told
him to simply hold out his hand. The raindrops broke
against his skin, the water flowed through his fingers,
and fell onto the ground. “You cannot yet end the storm,
young one, but the rain cannot deter you. To the clouds,
you are the unstoppable one.”

Seventy years later, the student would return to the
monastery. When the rain came, he stood beneath the
drops and, with a single punch, created a shockwave so
powerful it pushed the clouds from the sky above onto
neighboring lands. He remained unstoppable; the
clouds did not.

A Legendary Monk has spent a lifetime in
conversation with something most people ignore–the
current of energy that runs beneath muscle and bone
and breath, the fundamental force that connects a
mortal to the living cosmos. All is one, and one is all.
The clarity of meditation becomes understanding of the
highest order: there is a flame within all of us–one that
must be tended, if we dare to light it. The Monk moves
through reality the way water breaks down a rock–not
by force, but persistence. In a cosmos defined by the
devastations of hubris, the Monk is the rare thing that
does not need any of it, trusting in their own soul,
diminutive though it is compared to the world, and
achieving the greatest of powers through
enlightenment.

At 20th level, the Monk has gone so far past the limits
of the physical that they transcend what is possible.
They consciously move faster than reflex. They strike
with a precision that bypasses armor, resistance, and
even magic. They walk on thin air and control every
single fiber of their muscles. What defines them at this
tier is perfection. A chaos of movement, yet in absolute
control–the eye of something that, from the outside,
looks like a terrible storm.
Original PDF page 51

profound wholeness that rejuvenates and invigorates
your soul. You can enter this state as an action. For the

each of your turns. Once you enter this state, you cannot

Ki-Borne Awareness

At 22nd level, you learn to use your Focus to pierce
through any illusion and detect those that are hidden.
As a bonus action on your turn, you can expend 3 Focus
points to gain truesight out to a range of 30 feet.

You can expend additional Focus points beyond the
initial 3 to extend the range by 10 feet per additional
Focus point expended as part of the same bonus action.
This feature lasts for a minute or until you are

When you use this reaction, you can choose to
redirect the mitigated force toward a creature you can
see within 60 feet of you by expending 5 Focus points. If
you do so, that creature must succeed on a Dexterity
saving throw or take Soul damage equal to the damage
mitigated, up to a maximum of 100. You can expend
additional Focus points beyond the fifth to increase this
maximum. For each additional Focus point you expend
as part of this reaction, the maximum Soul damage the

Improved World Strike

When you reach 24th level, your World Strike damage
calculation is increased to 7d8 x 5, or 9d8 x 5 on a
critical hit.

Soulful Recovery

At 24th level, when you use the Recover action, you
regain 5 Focus points.

Power of the White Flame

White Flame of mortalkind. Your Focus Points are
increased to 30. Any feature or spell that would affect
your soul, such as the Soul Strike Mythical Technique,
automatically fails when targeting you, unless you
choose to be affected. Your maximum hit points cannot
be reduced in any way.

Legendary Paladin

Within the most illustrious convents in the world,
stalwart paladins share their quarters with luminous
clerics. There is great respect between these
professions, owing to how much they have in common,
but there is a fundamental difference. Whilst a cleric
draws power from devotion to a domain greater than
themselves, a paladin grows his might internally, from
the iron resolve contained in a promise–their sacred
oath. This is, perhaps, why they’ve garnered a
reputation for zealotry. The source of their power and
actions is deeply, inextricably personal to them. If it
weren’t so, radiance would not answer their call, their
hands would not heal the sick, and their voice would not
carry holy authority.

The sacred oath of a Legendary Paladin has become
potent and overwhelming. The laws derived from their
promise take hold in their vicinity, forcing their faithborne logic onto nearby beings. Those who follow a
Legendary Paladin’s tenets will be rewarded with
radiance and divine health, while those who defy them
will find themselves cursed and castigated. Such is the
extent to which their promise, which may seem a trifling
thing to those who’ve pledged themselves to great and
external forces, has become a real force in the world.
Like the proclamations of a deity, the oath of a
Legendary Paladin is enforced, not merely spoken or
threatened. Beyond 20th level, the space around them
functions with another fundamental law of reality–their
own code, added to the likes of gravity and time.

As anyone who’s been graced by their allyship will tell
you, it is practically impossible to surrender with the
faith of a Legendary Paladin on your side. It is a warm
hand on your shoulder, a bursting fire within your heart,
a trembling in each muscle. Fighting alongside a holy
warrior of this might is like sailing with the wind in your
favor.
